unitTitle: Y9 Applying programming skills with physical computing (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Hello physical world — meeting the micro:bit
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what the micro:bit is
I can list the micro:bit's input and output devices
I can write and run a Python program on the micro:bit
I can find and fix a syntax error
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: input · output · sensor · hardware component · flash · syntax error. TA: everyone has the Mu editor (or python.microbit.org) open + 1 micro:bit + USB cable per pair. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — input or output? (think–pair–share). On the starter worksheet pupils DRAG devices into input/output and DRAG labels onto the board (no writing).
   • Support: tick “an input is…”.  • Core: name one phone output.  • Challenge: is the display input or output, and how do you know? Likely error: calls a sensor just “input” → fix-words “which sensor — light, sound or movement?”
2. (10–25) Know your tools (I-do). Show the board front + back (embedded images); link each component back to the input/output columns. Pairs skim the component list.
3. (25–40) Walk-through — live-code “Hello there!”, flash it, watch it scroll (I-do → we-do). Pairs then change scroll→show and the text→Image.HEART (you-do).
   • Support: “change THIS line” arrow card.  • Core: change it unaided.  • Challenge: spot + fix the syntax error on the worksheet. Likely error: missing quotes → “text always goes inside quotes”. TA: “prompt, do not type it for them.”
4. (40–50) Pairs paste their share link + a screenshot on the activity worksheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pair shows their running board. TA notes who flashed a working program.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Bare bones — built-in input and output
lessons.objectives:
I can use the LED display for output
I can read a sensor or button for input
I can use a while True loop to keep checking for input
I can change a program to respond to a button press
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: output · input · sensor · while loop · pixel · coordinates. TA: 1 micro:bit + USB per pair; pairs swap driver/navigator each task. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what does each part do? Pupils MATCH each built-in part to its job (display=output; light/accelerometer=input).
   • Support: display is input or output? (tick).  • Core: name one input.  • Challenge: why a program needs both input and output.
2. (10–25) Output — the 5×5 display (I-do). show vs set_pixel; columns/rows numbered 0–4. Worked example, then pairs try the Output tasks.
   • Likely error: x or y above 4 → error scrolls; fix-words “the grid only goes up to 4”.
3. (25–40) Input — buttons + sensors (we-do). Predict then run the button counter; stress while True keeps checking and was_pressed counts each press once.
4. (40–50) You-do — on the worksheet, put the counter program in order (Parsons) then build it.
   • Support: block-order strip.  • Core: build unaided.  • Challenge: stop the count going above 4. Paste link + screenshot. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; collect project ideas on the board. TA notes who got input AND output working.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: micro:bit display (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Connections — pins and radio
lessons.objectives:
I can use a pin to send output (an LED or a speaker)
I can use a pin to read input (a switch or a touch)
I can send and receive a message by radio
I can explain why a circuit must be closed for current to flow
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: GPIO pin · circuit · current · wireless · radio · group · message. TA: pairs share micro:bits; some tasks need wires, a speaker or an LED; agree radio group numbers. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — light it up. Pupils ORDER the steps to close a circuit so an LED lights; link to science. Tick the power + ground pins on the board image.
   • Support: a circuit must be closed/open? (tick).  • Core: why GPIO pins are useful for a project.  • Challenge: an output use vs an input use of a pin.
2. (10–25) Pins / GPIO (I-do). Output through a pin (LED/speaker) vs input through a pin (switch/touch). Pairs try the Pins tasks; build the simple circuits from the worksheet.
   • Likely error: wrong pin (pin0 vs pin1) → fix-words “check which pin your wire is in”.
3. (25–40) Radio (I-do → we-do). radio.on / send / receive; same group can talk; messages are TEXT only (str(...) for numbers). Predict the heart-on-ping receiver, then run.
4. (40–50) You-do — sort pin uses (input/output), then put the “pass the love” radio program in order (Parsons) and test on two boards. Paste link + screenshot.
   • Support: block-order strip.  • Core: build it.  • Challenge: explain how shake-on-one shows heart-on-other. TA: “prompt, check connections first.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; set homework: put a project idea on paper. TA notes who got radio working.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound on slides.

resources.title: micro:bit pins (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Dream it up — designing a project
lessons.objectives:
I can describe a project for a real problem and audience
I can break my project into separate features
I can list the input, processing and output of a feature
I can choose the hardware components my project needs
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: design · problem · audience · decomposition · input · processing · output · prototype. TA: pairs now work together to the end of the unit; have the four example proposals + the project diary ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — input, processing or output? Pupils SORT the steps of a guessing game into the three parts (no writing).
   • Support: “show on the display” — which part? (tick).  • Core: write one input.  • Challenge: give the I/P/O of a feature.
2. (10–20) Four example projects (I-do). Clicker, guess-the-number, music keys, password-from-gestures. Reassure: a small finished idea beats a big unfinished one.
3. (20–35) How to design (we-do). Model on ONE example: problem → audience → features → input/processing/output → components. Decomposition = break the big idea into small features.
4. (35–50) You-do — on the worksheet, pick an idea, ORDER the design steps, then write the problem, audience and one decomposed feature; photo the proposal sheet for the screenshot box.
   • Support: match project → component.  • Core: problem + audience.  • Challenge: decompose a feature + list components. TA: “prompt for a SMALLER first feature.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pair pitches their project in one sentence. TA notes which pairs have a focused, buildable idea.
Design lesson — minimal code; differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Build it up — building the prototype
lessons.objectives:
I can build a prototype that follows my plan
I can use a "wait for the button" pattern
I can give and use peer feedback
I can record a problem and a fix in my diary
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: prototype · refine · feedback · decomposition · debug. TA: pairs need their proposal, code and project diary open; project parts and craft materials available. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — wait for the button. Pupils ORDER the flag/while program (Parsons) that waits for one press then stops; note alternative correct answers.
   • Support: the loop runs while flag is… (tick).  • Core: what makes the loop stop.  • Challenge: why “wait then stop” beats while True for starting a game.
2. (10–25) Build (you-do). Work from the proposal, ONE feature at a time, test on the board. This is the main assessment window — circulate and observe.
   • Likely stall: trying everything at once → fix-words “get ONE feature working first”. TA: “prompt, do not fix it for them.”
3. (25–35) Pause — feedback + diary (we-do). Tell another pair about your prototype; write their feedback and your response in the diary.
4. (35–50) Build again (you-do). Resume; plan what to finish, as only a little more time remains next lesson. Paste link + screenshot of the prototype so far.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…” + rate confidence (slider, uncredited); share what was hardest. TA notes pairs at risk of not finishing.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Wrap it up — finishing, reflecting, comparing
lessons.objectives:
I can finish and test my prototype against my plan
I can document what I built
I can reflect on a problem I solved and what I would change
I can compare the micro:bit with another platform
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: prototype · evaluation · rubric · microcontroller · microcomputer · platform. TA: pairs need code, board and project diary; have the rubric and the summative quiz ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — small board or full computer? Pupils SORT devices into microcontroller (micro:bit, Arduino) vs full computer (Raspberry Pi, laptop).
   • Support: the micro:bit runs… (tick).  • Core: name another platform.  • Challenge: why a Pi suits a job the micro:bit cannot.
2. (10–25) Finishing touches (you-do). Get the last feature working and test it against the plan. Use the rubric's Implementation/Ambition rows while pupils work.
3. (25–38) Document + reflect. Complete day 3 of the diary and the documentation boxes: what you built, problems solved, one top tip.
4. (38–50) Individual summative quiz (set separately) + plenary research: compare the micro:bit with the Raspberry Pi / Arduino — similarities and differences.
   • Support: match platform → description.  • Core: microcontroller vs full computer.  • Challenge: pick the platform for an HD-video project + why. TA: “prompt for honest reflection, not the ‘right’ answer.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…” + rate confidence (slider); each pair shows their finished prototype. TA notes completed projects for the rubric.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound on slides.
